name: Lake Crescent
short_description: Alpine lake rockhounding in the Olympic Mountains. Find agates, jasper, and chalcedony along shorelines and stream access points near this stunning glacial lake.
description:
Lake Crescent sits in Clallam County within the Olympic National Forest at 1,939 feet elevation. This pristine glacial lake is surrounded by steep forested terrain that makes shoreline rockhounding accessible from pullouts and designated access points. The area is known for agate and jasper specimens, particularly along the northern and eastern shores where wave action and seasonal water level changes expose fresh material.

The geology here reflects the Olympic Mountains' complex structure. Precambrian and Paleozoic metamorphic rocks, along with Tertiary marine sediments, create favorable conditions for chalcedony and agate formation. Streams feeding the lake have transported and polished stones that collect in gravel bars and beaches. The steep terrain means most productive rockhounding occurs within 50 feet of the waterline.

Access is primarily through the Olympic National Forest on the west side of the lake via US Highway 101. Several pullouts and day-use areas provide access for rockhounding. The terrain is steep and heavily forested, so expect rocky, uneven footing near the shoreline. Most productive hunting happens after winter storms or high water events when new material is exposed.

This location is best visited May through September when weather is stable and trail/road access is reliable. The lake itself remains accessible year-round, but winter rains and snow at elevation make access more challenging. Bring waterproof boots and be prepared for slippery terrain near the water's edge.
history: Lake Crescent was carved by glaciation during the Pleistocene epoch, with the lake basin occupying a U-shaped valley typical of glacial erosion in the Pacific Northwest. The surrounding Olympic Mountains are composed of uplifted Precambrian and Paleozoic metamorphic rocks that have been further altered by Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary activity, creating ideal conditions for secondary mineral formation including agate and chalcedony deposits.
tips: Search the shoreline gravels and stream beds immediately adjacent to the lake, especially where tributaries enter - these areas concentrate rounded agates and jasper. Use a small hand rake or classifier to work through beach material efficiently, and focus on areas with visible white or banded stones. Look for specimens in the 1-3 inch range; the polished stones here rarely exceed 4 inches. Early morning light along the northern shore makes spotting banded agates easier against the rocky beach.
terrain_notes: Steep, forested terrain at 1,939 feet elevation. Shoreline access from pullouts requires 50-200 feet of scrambling over rocky, often wet footing. Forest canopy is dense conifer (Douglas fir, western hemlock, western red cedar). Most rockhounding occurs within 50 feet of the water's edge on cobble and gravel beaches. Expect slippery rocks, uneven ground, and occasional deadfall.
best_season: May - September. Winter and early spring bring heavy rain and high water levels that limit safe shoreline access. Summer provides stable conditions, lower water levels that expose more beach material, and more predictable weather for hiking steep terrain.
written_directions: From Port Angeles, take US Highway 101 west for approximately 20 miles toward the town of Joyce. Lake Crescent will appear on your right (north side of the highway). Look for signed pullouts and day-use areas on the north shore. The most accessible rockhounding areas are near the Fairholme boat launch area and along the eastern arm of the lake accessible from Highway 101. Follow signs to any of the National Forest parking areas along the lake's perimeter.
road_conditions: US Highway 101 is fully paved. Pullouts and access roads are gravel or paved. No special clearance needed for passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Multiple gravel and paved pullouts along US Highway 101 on the north shore of Lake Crescent. Day-use parking areas near boat launches provide standard capacity. No reservation required. Parking is free.
collection_rules: Personal use only on National Forest lands. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Stay below the ordinary high water line. Respect private property boundaries where applicable - some shoreline may have private easements.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
Stay below ordinary high water line
Respect all posted closure areas
nearest_services: Port Angeles, approximately 20 miles east. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. Joyce (small town), approximately 5 miles west along Highway 101.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain. Rocky shoreline with uneven footing and occasional wet/slippery rocks. No wheelchair access to productive rockhounding areas. Steep terrain requires careful footing and hiking boots. Not suitable for limited mobility. Relatively short distances (under 200 feet) from parking to best collecting spots.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can you find at Lake Crescent in Washington?
faq.answer: Lake Crescent is known for banded agates with white, gray, and brown coloration, plus red and brown jasper specimens. Most finds are in the 1-3 inch range and have been naturally polished by wave action. Chalcedony pieces and quartz are also common. The best specimens show visible banding or distinct color patterns.

faq.question: Is Lake Crescent free rockhounding or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Lake Crescent is on National Forest land and rockhounding is completely free with no permit required. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use. There are no fees for parking or access at the pullouts along US Highway 101.

faq.question: Where exactly should you look for rocks at Lake Crescent shoreline?
faq.answer: Focus on the shoreline gravels and beach areas, especially where streams and tributaries feed into the lake. The northern shore and eastern arm near the Fairholme area are most productive. Look in areas with visible concentrations of rounded stones and cobbles, particularly after winter storms when new material is exposed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Lake Crescent?
faq.answer: May through September is ideal. Winter brings heavy rain and high water levels that make shoreline access dangerous and limit exposed material. Summer provides lower water levels that expose more beach, stable weather, and easier hiking on the steep terrain surrounding the lake.

faq.question: How difficult is the terrain for rockhounding at Lake Crescent near Port Angeles?
faq.answer: The terrain is moderate difficulty. While distances from parking to rockhounding areas are short (50-200 feet), the footing is rocky and often wet or slippery. You need sturdy waterproof hiking boots and comfort on uneven ground. The forest terrain is steep but not extreme for most hikers in decent shape.

faq.question: Is Lake Crescent a good rockhounding spot for beginners with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Lake Crescent is beginner and family-friendly. The collecting is straightforward - look for colorful stones in the beach gravels. Kids ages 8+ can easily participate with adult supervision. Keep trips short due to steep terrain and slippery rocks. Always supervise children near the water and watch for unstable banks.

meta_description: Find agates, jasper, and chalcedony at Lake Crescent in Clallam County. Free National Forest rockhounding near Port Angeles. 25 lb daily limit.
